dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to the address for a reported seizure.
Hi, I'm Medic 90. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I feel like I'm going to have a seizure. It's been coming on for a while now.
George, can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is George, and we're at my house on Redacted Street.
What date is it today? And do you remember when you originally called?
It's the 17th of May, I believe. I don't recall the exact time.
Are there other symptoms you're currently experiencing?
I have a headache, back pain, arm and leg pain. Also a metallic taste in my mouth. No shortness of breath.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What happened before the symptoms started showing up?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I've been using a lot of vodka instead of my Keppra lately. Ever since I stopped my Keppra, I've been feeling this way.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain, how bad is your pain right now?
It's about a 5 - not terrible but definitely present.
We'll do a quick check to see how you're breathing. Ready?
Yep, go ahead.
I'm going to put a fingertip oximeter on you to check your oxygen level.
Your oxygen level is 93%.
We'll take your blood pressure now. Just extend your arm.
Blood pressure is 155 over 89.
Can you take a deep breath in for me?
You're breathing at 26 times per minute.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Oxygen remains at 93%- a bit low, but okay for now.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Your pulse is 141 and quite fast right now.
Blood pressure is now 155 over 89.

dialogue.micro_intent: check respiratory status with degree of effort
dialogue.utterance: Your breathing seems regular and not difficult.
Let me check your responsiveness. Can you open your eyes now?
(opens eyes)
Great. Now, can you tell me your name?
George.
Mr. George, you're alert, so your GCS score is 15.
I'm placing you on an ECG monitor so we can check your heart rhythm.
Your EKG is showing sinus tachycardia. Monitor is active.
I'll take a quick blood sugar reading now.
Blood sugar is 98 - within the normal range.
I noticed you mentioned a few medical conditions. Can you tell me more?
I have epilepsy and am seen by a neurologist for it. I also have a history of tachycardia and anxiety.
Can you tell me what medications you take regularly?
I take Metoprolol, Keppra, and Ativan. I'm knowledgeable of drug allergies - I don't have any known drug allergy.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Your pupils are equal, round, and slowly reacting to light. 40. Secondary Assessment; skin exam - Color: Jaundice, pallor, etc.; Partner: Your skin is hot and dry without signs of jaundice or pallor.
Abdomen is soft and distended with no tenderness.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Mr. George, can you move your arms and legs without trouble?

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: I can move everything fine.
Your heart rate has dropped a bit to 127 - still fast, but a decrease.
Your breaths remain at 26 - still consistent.
Blood pressure is now 146 over 105 - diastolic increased slightly.
Oxygen is now at 89% - slightly lower. We'll monitor it closely.
Your blood sugar is now at 0, but we believe this is a machine error. The dispenser is working correctly, as prior glucose readings were valid.
EKG remains stable - still showing sinus tachycardia with no significant changes so far.
We did another reading. EKG is still somewhat abnormal, and the 12-lead showed possible anterior infarct in V2.
We'll now secure you into the stretcher in a semi-Fowler's position for comfort.
Movement complete and you're now fully stabilized.
Let's take one last check - blood pressure is 115 over 86, oxygen is at 95%, and your respiratory rate is 17 - trending better. R set?
Everything is trending better. Mr. George is clear for transport.
We'll get you into the ambulance now.
Transport has begun.
As per the protocol, we placed you on continuous four-lead ECG monitoring as early as arrival.
We've also completed a 12-lead ECG that confirmed sinus tachycardia with no elevation but did note a possible anterior infarct on V2.
We'll transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] for a full workup, including looking into any cardiovascular concerns like the possible anterior infarct. Sound okay?
Yeah, that works. Just take me there.
We're carefully transferring you onto the stretcher.
All straps are secured, and we're ready to move.
We are now en route to HTCH-Parham.
I'll check once more. Glucose reading still comes back at 0 - we are monitoring and ensuring hydration remains stable through IV access.
Current pulse is 126 - consistent with a trending relief in tachycardia.
Oxygen level bumping up to 95% - on the right track.
EKG still consistent - sinus tachycardia with episodes non-threatening observed.
How is the pain now on that 0 to 10 scale?
Still a 5, but feelin' a bit easier with the supportive care.
We've secured all vitals and readied the full ECG. Destination ready.
Vitals are stable. I confirm we are ready for hospital hand-off.
Can you clarify if you are currently taking any other drugs not previously mentioned?
Nope, just the ones I told you. Times have been rough, so I've been substituting for my medication - vodka instead of Keppra since it was the only thing around.
And tell me, when was the last time you ate or drank before today's episode?
I had some food and drank last, maybe six hours ago. Vodka, again, been substituting again.
Based on the history, vitals, and our ongoing assessment, you are currently believed to be experiencing alcohol-related withdrawal with a seizure prodrome.
Under protocol 3-12: medical-seizure, you are in a semi-Fowler's position on oxygen via nasal cannula.
Everything is secured and stable. I repeat: destination is [COMPANY_NAME] - Parham.
The nasal cannula is delivering 4 LPM as per protocol.
ECG functionality confirmed - continuous and stable monitoring in place.
No medications administered, at the patient's request - they are clear on their refusal for sedatives.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - mental status assessment

dialogue.utterance: Good. Last thing: where are you right now and what streets were mentioned in your location?
Redacted Street - can't recall the full name but I'm pretty sure I'm on that block.

On our way to Parham.
O2 flow confirmed and optimal.

No strict schedule now - but before life was better, the pattern was once a day. Now it's whenever and as much as I can get.
